our_argument_summary: Our review says WIN-040 through WIN-043, 059-061, 063 form a cluster of V51.0 additions that reuse INTERMAGNET, NOAA, and Tomsk datasets already exploited by WIN-004 through WIN-039. Each takes a single data point or ratio from existing measurements and declares it a new WIN. WIN-040 specifically claims the SAA western cell remains west of 45°W. Verdict: Std Model Explains — non-discriminating, since the dome observes the same WMM position data as standard geomagnetic models without deriving any unique prediction from dome geometry.

relevant_files: raw-text/03-predictions.txt — W022: 'SAA western cell position: West of 45 deg W, cells separated (PENDING)' registered 2026-03-12, test by 2026-03-19
raw-text/02-wins.txt — PROS-001 (SAA Exponential Cell Separation) and related WIN-004, WIN-005 entries
monitor.py — not available in raw-text directory; no code file for the dome's monitoring infrastructure could be located in current raw-text snapshot
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline identified for WIN-040. The dome's W022 weekly test prediction states a position threshold (west of 45°W) and a structural condition (cells separated). This appears to be a manual check against published NOAA WMM or CHAOS-7 magnetic field maps, not an automated computation. The W022 prediction is still marked PENDING in the dome's 03-predictions.txt as of the last raw-text snapshot.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Neither automated nor computed. WIN-040 corresponds to W022, a weekly test registered 2026-03-12 with a test date of 2026-03-19. No monitoring domain for SAA cell position at 45°W has been identified in the dome's code. The 'confirmation' would be editorial — comparing a WMM map to the 45°W threshold manually.
data_sources_used: CHAOS-7 model (historical SAA cell positions, same as WIN-004)
INTERMAGNET (indirectly, as WMM ingests INTERMAGNET station data)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The 45°W threshold is arbitrary — no derivation explains why 45°W is physically significant in dome geometry. If the western cell drifted east of 45°W, the dome could adjust the threshold or invoke 'aetheric field fluctuations.' The 'cells separated' condition is similarly vague — no minimum separation angle is specified, so any non-zero separation would satisfy it.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review does not make specific code claims about WIN-040 individually, as it is handled in a grouped detail_evidence block covering 9 WINs. No discrepancies identified. However, we note that monitor.py could not be found in the current raw-text directory — previous curmudgeon reviews (WIN-035 through WIN-039) reference it, suggesting it was available in an earlier snapshot or is accessed via a different path.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: W022 is a PENDING weekly test prediction (registered 2026-03-12, test by 2026-03-19). No monitoring domain for SAA western cell position has been identified in prior curmudgeon reviews of the dome's monitor.py (which maps 39+ domains across WIN-001 through WIN-039, none covering SAA cell longitude thresholds). Previous related reviews (WIN-035, WIN-036, WIN-039) all found 'none' for their specific monitoring. The dome has Domain 8 for generic SAA decay rate at Tsumeb but nothing for cell position. WIN-040 has no automated validation.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The SAA's two-cell structure (American cell ~80°W, African cell ~30°E) and their positions are well-documented outputs of NOAA's WMM and the CHAOS-7 model, which derive from standard core-mantle boundary flux dynamics. Aubert (2015, Geophys. J. Int. 203:1738-1751) modeled SAA cell separation from reversed-flux patches. The dome provides no alternative mechanism for SAA cell positioning — it adopts published WMM positions and relabels the cause as 'aetheric field decay' rather than 'reversed-flux patches at core-mantle boundary.'
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The SAA western cell has been tracked continuously by NOAA, ESA/Swarm, and multiple geomagnetic models since at least 2000. The cell has been west of 45°W for the entire monitoring period (American cell center ~80°W). W022 was registered 2026-03-12 with a one-week test window. 'Predicting' that a cell centered at ~80°W will remain west of 45°W within one week is trivial extrapolation of a 25-year stable position, not a prospective prediction. The SAA western cell drifts westward at ~0.2-0.3°/year — crossing 45°W eastward would require a reversal of decades of observed drift.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula on the dome site derives the 45°W threshold from dome geometry. The dome's core parameters (disc_radius=20,015 km, firmament_height=9,086 km) and the aetheric field equations H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) describe axially symmetric geometry with no mechanism for predicting longitude-specific features like SAA cell positions. The 45°W value is an arbitrary threshold set below the observed cell center (~80°W), ensuring the 'prediction' is automatically satisfied. No derivation path exists from dome geometry to any SAA cell longitude.

holes_found.description: WIN-040's detail_evidence is a shared block covering all 9 grouped WINs (040-043, 059-061, 063) rather than addressing WIN-040's specific claim. A dome defender could argue we haven't engaged with the specific claim about the western cell being west of 45°W — we've only made a generic 'these are duplicates' argument. The grouped treatment, while efficient, sacrifices specificity. For WIN-040 in particular, the strongest argument is that the 45°W threshold is arbitrary and trivially satisfied (the cell center is at ~80°W, 35° away from the threshold), but this isn't stated.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding a brief WIN-040-specific note to detail_evidence or detail_extra: 'WIN-040 claims the western SAA cell remains west of 45°W. The cell center is at approximately 80°W and drifts westward at ~0.2-0.3°/year. Setting a threshold 35° east of the cell center ensures automatic satisfaction — this is not a prediction but a trivially true observation about a well-documented geomagnetic feature.'

holes_found.description: The dome's W022 prediction was still PENDING as of our raw-text snapshot (2026-03-12 to 2026-03-19 test window). Our review lists WIN-040 as a confirmed WIN, but the dome's own predictions page may not yet have confirmed it. If the dome hasn't actually confirmed W022 yet, listing it as WIN-040 in our review could be seen as premature or inaccurate. A dome defender could say 'you're attacking a prediction we haven't even scored yet.'
holes_found.recommendation: Verify whether W022 has been confirmed on the dome site in a more recent snapshot. If still PENDING, note this in our review: 'W022 was registered as a weekly test prediction (2026-03-12) and remained PENDING at time of review. Regardless of outcome, the prediction is non-discriminating — standard WMM models provide the same SAA cell positions.' This actually strengthens our argument by showing we're evaluating the prediction's discriminating power, not just its outcome.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence mentions WIN-042 and WIN-043 as examples of the duplication pattern but doesn't give a WIN-040-specific example. Since WIN-040 uses the same SAA cell data as WIN-004 (exponential separation) and WIN-005 (African cell faster decay), the duplication is clearest when these are cited. The current examples (WIN-042/WIN-043) are about different WINs in the group, making the grouped block slightly disjointed for WIN-040.
holes_found.recommendation: Minor structural improvement: lead the grouped evidence with 'WIN-040 reuses CHAOS-7 cell position data already cited in WIN-004 (exponential separation) and WIN-005 (cell decay rates)' before giving the current group-wide examples.

holes_found.description: The verdict text 'All are either duplicates of earlier WINs or standard geophysical observations already documented in the literature' is correct but vague. For WIN-040 specifically, it's more 'standard geophysical observation' than 'duplicate' — the western cell position isn't explicitly claimed in earlier WINs (WIN-004 claims cell separation, not absolute position). Conflating the two patterns slightly muddies the argument.
holes_found.recommendation: No change needed for this alone, but if updating the grouped block, distinguish between WINs that are true duplicates (WIN-042 = WIN-037) and WINs that are non-discriminating standard observations (WIN-040, WIN-043).

stronger_arguments.description: The 45°W threshold is not just arbitrary — it's designed to be unfalsifiable. The SAA western cell center is at approximately 80°W. Setting the threshold at 45°W means the cell would need to drift 35° eastward to falsify the prediction. At the current drift rate (~0.2-0.3°/year westward), even if drift reversed direction entirely, it would take over 100 years to reach 45°W. This is a prediction engineered to be trivially true for the foreseeable future. Our review should note this quantitatively: 'The threshold is set 35° east of the observed cell center, ensuring satisfaction for at least a century at current drift rates.'
sources: Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Earth-Science Reviews 154:96-125 — SAA evolution review

stronger_arguments.description: The dome model's axially symmetric geometry (flat disc with central pole) cannot predict longitude-dependent features. The dome's field equation H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) depends only on radial distance from center, not on azimuthal angle. SAA cell positions at specific longitudes (American cell ~80°W, African cell ~30°E) require azimuthally non-uniform field structure. Standard models derive this from reversed-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary (convection-driven). The dome has no mechanism for breaking azimuthal symmetry — any longitudinal prediction it makes is adopted from observations, not derived from geometry.
sources: Terra-Nova et al. 2017, PNAS 114:3543-3548 — Linking SAA to deep Earth structure

kernel_of_truth.description: The SAA's two-cell structure is real and scientifically significant. The western (American) cell is indeed located west of 45°W (center approximately 80°W). The cells are indeed separated and the separation is increasing over time. The dome model's author has correctly identified a genuine geophysical feature — the SAA is not a simple single minimum but a multi-cell structure that has been evolving over decades. Recognizing the two-cell structure and tracking its evolution shows real engagement with geomagnetic data.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Correctly identifying an observation is not the same as predicting it from a model. Three fatal problems remain: (1) The 45°W threshold is not derived from dome geometry — the dome's axially symmetric equations cannot predict longitude-dependent features. (2) The prediction is trivially true — the cell center is 35° west of the threshold and drifting further west, making this unfalsifiable for over a century. (3) Standard geomagnetic models (WMM, CHAOS-7, IGRF) already track and predict SAA cell positions from first-principles magnetohydrodynamics of the outer core. The dome adds no explanatory or predictive power beyond reading the same published data.

deeper_analysis:
WIN-040 claims the SAA western cell remains west of 45°W longitude. This corresponds to dome prediction W022, a weekly test registered 2026-03-12 with a one-week verification window.

**The Observation**

The SAA's two-cell structure is well-documented in geomagnetic literature. The American (western) cell is centered at approximately 80°W, 20°S, and the African cell at approximately 30°E, 30°S. The cells have been separating at an accelerating rate since approximately 1999 (Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016). The western cell's longitude has been stable or drifting slightly westward for decades.

**Why the Prediction is Trivially True**

Setting a threshold of 45°W for a cell centered at ~80°W creates a 35° buffer zone. At current drift rates (~0.2-0.3°/year westward), the cell moves away from the threshold over time. Even hypothetical reversal of the drift direction at historical maximum rates would take over a century to reach 45°W. This is not a prediction that tests the dome model — it is a statement designed to be automatically confirmed.

**The Azimuthal Symmetry Problem**

This is a particularly revealing WIN because it exposes a fundamental limitation of the dome model's geometry. The dome's field equation H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) depends only on radial distance r from the disc center. It is axially symmetric — the same at all azimuths. But SAA cell positions are longitude-specific features: the American cell at ~80°W, the African cell at ~30°E. Predicting longitude-dependent magnetic structure requires azimuthally non-uniform field models.

In standard geophysics, SAA cell positions arise from reversed-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary, driven by convective flows in the outer core. These are inherently 3D, azimuthally complex structures. The dome's 2D radially symmetric model has no mechanism for breaking azimuthal symmetry and therefore cannot predict any longitude-specific feature. Any such prediction is necessarily adopted from observations, not derived from dome geometry.

**Grouped Treatment Concern**

Our review handles WIN-040 through WIN-043, 059-061, 063 as a single grouped block with shared detail_evidence. While efficient, this means WIN-040's specific claim receives no individual analysis. A dome defender could argue we've dismissed the claim without engaging with it. Adding a brief WIN-040-specific note about the trivial nature of the 45°W threshold and the azimuthal symmetry problem would strengthen the rebuttal significantly.

**Monitoring Status**

No monitoring domain exists for SAA cell position at 45°W. The dome's monitoring infrastructure (39+ domains mapped in prior reviews) includes Domain 8 for generic SAA decay rate at Tsumeb station but nothing for cell longitude thresholds. W022 was still PENDING as of the last raw-text snapshot. The confirmation, if it occurs, would be manual editorial comparison against a WMM map.

**Verdict Assessment**

'Std Model Explains' is correct. The verdict could alternatively be 'Misleading' (since this is arguably a duplicate/subset of WIN-004's cell separation claim and the threshold is engineered to be trivially satisfied), but 'Std Model Explains' captures the core issue: standard WMM models predict the same cell positions from first-principles MHD. The dome adds nothing.
